skill.name: api-tester
skill.description: Test REST, GraphQL, authentication, response validation, and API performance with practical request examples.
skill.summary: Generate practical API tests for endpoints, auth flows, schemas, errors, and load behavior.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution findings are mostly false positives from Markdown fences and example commands, not hidden executable scripts. Network usage is intentional for an API testing skill, but generated curl, fetch, requests, ab, and Artillery examples can hit real services, mutate data, expose tokens, or create load if used carelessly.

medium_findings.title: Network Requests and Mutating API Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill provides curl, fetch, axios, requests, and load-test examples for real HTTP endpoints. This is legitimate for API testing, but agents could execute requests that create, update, delete, or stress resources if users do not restrict targets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network and shell command examples are explicit and aligned with the skill purpose. Risk depends on whether the generated commands are run against authorized staging targets.

medium_findings.title: Credential Exposure in Request Examples
medium_findings.description: Authentication examples place bearer tokens, API keys, Basic Auth values, and OAuth client secrets in commands and request snippets. The API-key-in-query example can leak credentials through logs, browser history, proxies, or monitoring tools.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential-bearing examples are directly present, including an API key in a query parameter. They use placeholders, so this is unsafe guidance risk rather than confirmed exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static Code Execution Findings Are Mostly Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many fenced code blocks and Postman script strings as shell or Ruby execution. These are instructional examples, not packaged scripts that execute automatically when the skill loads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is clearly inside Markdown examples or JSON collection text. No separate executable file or automatic launch path was found in the analyzed file.

low_findings.title: Dual-Use Security Test Guidance
low_findings.description: The skill recommends SQL injection, XSS, authentication, CORS, and rate-limit tests. This is normal API security testing guidance, but users should only apply it to systems they own or are authorized to test.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The testing guidance is explicit and dual-use, but there is no evidence of malicious targeting or evasion instructions. The risk is misuse by an operator.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Finding Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The static env_access finding points to an API key query parameter example, not an environment variable read. The skill later recommends environment variables for tokens, which is safer guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 317 contains a placeholder query parameter, not process environment access. The remaining notes advise safer token handling and avoiding committed secrets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Load Testing Commands Against HTTP Targets
dangerous_patterns.description: Apache Bench and Artillery examples can generate sustained traffic. Users should run them only against approved environments with rate limits and rollback plans.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The load-testing commands are explicit and can affect service availability if executed against production. They are legitimate but operationally risky.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Key in Query Parameter
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes an API key query string example. Query parameters are commonly logged, so header-based or secret-manager based approaches are safer.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The query parameter example is directly visible and uses an API key placeholder. This is a well-known credential leakage pattern even without real secrets.

content.value_statement: Manual API checks can miss errors in authentication, schemas, and failure paths. This skill helps generate repeatable tests for REST, GraphQL, performance, and security scenarios.
actual_capabilities: Creates curl examples for GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, and GraphQL requests.
Generates JavaScript and Python API test patterns using common HTTP clients.
Outlines Postman collection structure with variables, bearer auth, and test scripts.
Provides load testing examples with Apache Bench and Artillery.
Covers response schema checks, status codes, authentication failures, and rate limits.
Lists security test scenarios for authorization, input validation, CORS, and XSS checks.
limitations: It does not verify that a target API is authorized for testing.
It provides examples and templates, not a managed test runner.
Generated requests may need changes for each API contract and environment.
Load testing examples can harm production systems if used without limits.

use_cases.title: Validate Endpoint Behavior
use_cases.description: Create repeatable checks for status codes, headers, response fields, and common error cases.

use_cases.title: Build QA Test Collections
use_cases.description: Draft Postman, JavaScript, or Python tests for regression coverage across API workflows.

use_cases.title: Review API Security Controls
use_cases.description: Plan authorized checks for authentication, authorization, input validation, CORS, and rate limiting.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Endpoint Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Create API tests for GET /api/users. Include status code checks, headers, and expected response fields.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Authenticated Requests
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate safe authenticated request examples for this endpoint. Use placeholders for secrets and avoid putting keys in URLs.

prompt_templates.title: Build Regression Test Cases
prompt_templates.prompt: Create positive and negative API test cases for this user workflow. Include validation errors, unauthorized access, and duplicate records.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Controlled Load Testing
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a staged load test for this API. Include target limits, success metrics, rollback conditions, and safe test environment assumptions.

output_examples.input: Test the user listing endpoint with bearer authentication.
output_examples.output: A concise test plan with an authenticated request, expected 200 response, required fields, and unauthorized-token checks.

output_examples.input: Create security checks for a login endpoint.
output_examples.output: A list of authorized tests for invalid credentials, missing fields, rate limits, error messages, and token handling.

output_examples.input: Prepare a load test for a staging API.
output_examples.output: A staged performance plan with warm-up traffic, steady-state load, response-time goals, and stop conditions.

best_practices: Use staging or development APIs unless production testing is explicitly approved.
Keep secrets in environment variables or secret managers, never in URLs or committed files.
Set rate limits, timeouts, and rollback conditions before running load tests.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated mutating requests against production without approval.
Do not paste real API keys, bearer tokens, or client secrets into prompts.
Do not use load testing commands on third-party systems without written permission.

faq.question: Can this skill call APIs directly?
faq.answer: It can generate commands and code that call APIs. Execution depends on the host tool and user approval.

faq.question: Is it safe for production APIs?
faq.answer: Use it carefully. Prefer staging, read-only tests, and explicit approval for production targets.

faq.question: Does it support GraphQL?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes GraphQL query and mutation testing examples with variables and authentication headers.

faq.question: Can it generate Postman collections?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes collection structure, variables, bearer authentication, and response test scripts.

faq.question: How should secrets be handled?
faq.answer: Use placeholders in prompts and store real tokens in secure environment variables or secret managers.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate, but it can generate network, mutation, credential, and load-test commands.
